title: Coil-built decorative lidded jar
subtitle: Wide-shouldered storage jar with stacked coils and a slab push-in lid
excerpt: A wide-shouldered jar built from stacked coils of air-dry clay, with the exterior coil ridges left intentionally visible as decoration. A flat slab lid with a pinched knob completes the piece. No kiln is needed. Finished dimensions are approximately 150 mm tall and 120 mm across the shoulder.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on coil-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rolled rope of clay used as the primary building unit in coil hand-building, stacked in layers to build up the walls of a form
A creamy mixture of clay and water used as an adhesive to join coils or other clay surfaces together
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together to create a strong bond
The stage at which clay has dried enough to hold its shape but remains workable enough to be carved or joined
The stage at which all moisture has left the clay body, leaving it pale, room-temperature, and ready for surface treatment
Fully dried but unfired clay work; fragile and soluble in water until sealed
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used here for the jar base and push-in lid

content.text: This jar is built from stacked
coils
of air-dry clay. The inside of each wall layer is blended smooth for strength; the outside coil ridges are left visible as decorative banding. A flat
slab

lid with a pinched knob handle completes the piece. No kiln is required. The finished jar is approximately 150 mm tall with a 120 mm shoulder diameter. It is suitable as a desk tidy, jewellery storage, or decorative jar.
Preparation
Prepare a pot of
slip

by dissolving a walnut-sized piece of clay in a small amount of water until the mixture reaches the consistency of thick yogurt. Knead the working clay for two to three minutes before starting. Work on a wooden board and keep unused clay covered with a damp cloth. Plan the proportions of the jar before starting: a wide shoulder requires coils to expand outward over the first third of the height, hold steady through the middle, then contract inward at the neck.
Construction

Make the base. Roll a slab 8 mm thick and cut a circle 120 mm in diameter for the base. Set this on the wooden board; it will remain on the board while you build up the walls. Smooth the top surface of the base with a damp sponge.

Roll the first coil. Roll a rope of clay on the board using both hands, working from the centre outward until the coil is approximately 12 mm in diameter and 380 mm long (enough to travel once around the 120 mm base with some overlap). All coils in this jar are 12 mm in diameter for consistent wall thickness.
Attach the first coil to the base using the
score-and-slip

method: scratch cross-hatch marks around the outer edge of the base top surface using the needle tool, apply slip to the scored area, then press the coil firmly around the perimeter. The join at the start and end of the coil should also be scored and slipped. Blend the coil downward into the base on the interior using a wooden rib or your fingertip to create a strong interior joint.

Continue adding coils, one at a time. Score the top of the previous coil, apply slip, then press the new coil on top. After each coil, blend the interior join downward with a rib or finger. Leave the exterior join unblended. The coil ridges on the outside are the decorative texture of this piece.

Shape the body. For the first six coils, position each coil slightly wider than the one below to build the expanding shoulder. For the next two coils, keep the same diameter. For the top three coils, position each coil slightly inward to bring the neck in to approximately 80 mm diameter. Use calipers or a ruler to measure the diameter at each level.
Allow the lower half of the jar to firm to the
leather-hard
stage before adding the upper coils. A column of wet coils will sag under its own weight. Once the lower section has firmed (4-6 hours), resume building upward.
Finish the top rim. Lay the final coil and blend it smooth on both inside and outside for a clean rim. Use a damp sponge to even out the rim height, checking from above that it sits level.

Make the lid. Roll a slab 6 mm thick and cut an octagonal or circular piece 5 mm larger in diameter than the neck opening, so it rests on the rim rather than falling through. Mix a small amount of slip and prepare a marble-sized ball of clay for the knob. Score and slip the centre top of the lid. Pinch the clay ball into a dome shape and attach it to the lid using score-and-slip. Smooth the join at the base of the knob.

Test the lid fit at the leather-hard stage. The lid should sit flat on the rim with a small overhang around the edge. If calipers are available, measure the neck diameter and the lid diameter to confirm the 5 mm overhang all round.
Drying
Allow the jar to dry slowly at room temperature. The coil-built structure will take 48-3 days to reach the
bone-dry
stage, longer in humid conditions. Dry the lid separately on a flat surface. The
greenware
stage is the period of greatest fragility. Do not lift the jar by its rim; always support it from below. Keep the jar away from direct heat sources during drying.
Finishing

Sand any rough exterior ridges lightly with fine sandpaper if a softer texture is preferred, or leave the coil ridges as-is for the intended decorative effect. Paint with acrylic paints and allow to dry fully. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to all surfaces inside and outside the jar and both faces of the lid. Let each coat dry fully before applying the next.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. This jar is not suitable as a water vessel. It is suitable for dry storage: desk supplies, jewellery, dried botanicals. Do not store damp items inside. Wipe the exterior with a dry or barely damp cloth. Keep indoors away from sustained humidity.
Variations
Geometric coil decoration: leave the exterior coil ridges unblended. Then use the needle tool to incise fine-line geometric motifs between the coil bands on the leather-hard surface.

Flat-top lidded jar: omit the knob and make a smooth, flat slab lid. Add a small finger notch by pressing the fettling knife handle into the rim of the lid before drying so it can be lifted off without a projecting knob.
Tall cylinder jar: keep all coils the same diameter rather than shaping outward and inward. This produces a straight-sided cylinder jar about 180 mm tall by 80 mm across.

rows.problem: Coil joints crack as the jar dries
rows.cause: Insufficient slip at the join, or coils added when the previous one was too dry
rows.fix: At the leather-hard stage, fill cracks with slip using a small brush and smooth over. At bone-dry, fill with undiluted PVA and allow to cure before painting

rows.problem: Jar sags or leans to one side
rows.cause: Too many wet coils added before the lower section had time to firm
rows.fix: Support the leaning side with a wad of scrunched paper while the clay firms; check vertical alignment with a ruler against the side of the piece

rows.problem: Lid does not sit flat on the rim
rows.cause: The rim is not level, or the lid warped during drying
rows.fix: At leather-hard, press the rim gently with a damp finger to level it. For a warped lid, press it flat under a weight on a flat surface while it is still at leather-hard

rows.cause: Rib tool dragged too sharply across the clay
rows.fix: Dampen the rib tool before each pass and use long sweeping strokes at a low angle to the wall surface rather than pressing inward

rows.problem: Base separates from the first coil
rows.cause: Score-and-slip was not applied before attaching the coil, or the base had already started to stiffen
rows.fix: Re-score both surfaces, apply fresh slip, press firmly together, and support the join with a thin coil of clay on the interior
